summaryTemplate: {{className}} is the {{patternRole}}. It exposes the same interface as the real subject and forwards calls to it, with room to gate, defer, or replace the call. Pattern body lives at {{file}}:{{lineStart}}-{{lineEnd}}.
chosenRationaleTemplate: Tagged as Proxy because {{className}} stands in for the real subject behind the same interface — callers can substitute one for the other. The structural fingerprint is the matching interface plus a held real-subject reference; the *intent* is access control, lazy initialization, or remote stand-in rather than behavior extension.

proxy_class.note: Proxy class — drop-in replacement for the real subject from the caller's perspective. That signature equivalence is what makes the proxy invisible at the call site.

proxy_real_subject.note: Real subject reference — the proxy may construct it lazily, hold it forever, or replace it on demand. The choice of *when* to materialize this reference is usually where Proxy's value lives.

proxy_forwarding_op.note: Proxy operation — same signature as the real subject's. Anything the proxy adds (caching, permission checks, remote call, logging) wraps the forwarding line below.

proxy_forwarded_call.note: Real subject invocation — the actual work happens here, not in the proxy. If the proxy short-circuits (e.g. returns cached data without forwarding), that decision is the proxy's job, not the real subject's.

runnerUpComparisonTemplates.Decorator: Decorator and Proxy share the wrap-with-same-interface shape. The difference is intent: Decorator *adds behavior* on top of the wrapped call; Proxy *controls access* to the wrapped call. {{className}} reads as the latter — gating or substituting rather than augmenting.
runnerUpComparisonTemplates.Adapter: Adapter exposes a *different* interface than the wrapped class. Proxy preserves the interface so it can be substituted transparently; that signature equivalence at lines {{lineStart}}-{{lineEnd}} is the Proxy signal.
